---
title: "Optimizing Under Equality Constraints: Lagrange Multipliers"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Optimization track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE   = "#2b3a67"    # objective / level sets of f
CONSTR  = "#c1121f"    # the constraint curve / surface
GRADF   = "#0a7d4d"    # gradient of f
GRADG   = "#b8860b"    # gradient of g
GREY    = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the first article of the Optimization
> track's **constrained-optimization** branch. Until now every minimization has been
> *free*: choose any $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ you like and drive the objective down.
> Here the decision variable is **fenced in** — it must lie on a constraint surface
> $g(\mathbf{x})=0$ — and the entire derivative test has to be rebuilt to respect that
> fence. It assumes [OPTM-02 *Minimizing in Many Variables*] for the **gradient**
> $\nabla f$, the reading of $\nabla f$ as the **direction of steepest ascent**, the
> **stationarity** condition $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ (the *unconstrained* test we are about
> to amend), and the **Hessian** for second-order checks. From the *Geometry of Data /
> Applied Linear Algebra* track it borrows one geometric fact, *used* and not re-derived:
> **the gradient $\nabla g$ is perpendicular to the level set $g=\text{const}$** — we
> restate and motivate it inline so the argument never leans on an unseen result.
>
> **What it sets up.** It is the foundation for [OPTM-14 *Inequality Constraints and the
> KKT Conditions*] (what changes when the fence is a one-sided $g(\mathbf{x})\le 0$) and
> [OPTM-15 *Duality*] (the multiplier reread as the variable of a twin problem). The
> **shadow-price** interpretation derived here is the seed that makes both of those click.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

We are asked to minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ not over all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ but only over the
points that satisfy an equality constraint $g(\mathbf{x})=0$ — a curve, a surface, a
budget line. The unconstrained test "set $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$" is now *wrong*: the
unconstrained minimum almost never sits on the constraint surface, and forcing the
gradient to vanish ignores the fence entirely. This article rebuilds the optimality
condition from the geometry up. The single governing fact, derived from a picture before
any symbol, is that **at a constrained optimum the gradient of the objective is parallel
to the gradient of the constraint**, $\nabla f = \lambda\,\nabla g$ — because any
component of $\nabla f$ *along* the surface would be a direction you could still slide to
improve. The scalar $\lambda$ that buys that parallelism is the **Lagrange multiplier**,
and we package the whole condition into one scalar function, the **Lagrangian**
$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\lambda)=f(\mathbf{x})-\lambda\,g(\mathbf{x})$, whose ordinary
stationarity $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}=\mathbf{0},\ \partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\lambda=0$
reproduces both the parallelism *and* the constraint. We extend it to several
constraints, prove the **multiplier is a shadow price** — $\lambda$ equals the rate at
which the optimal value improves as the constraint is relaxed — and carry the machinery
through three honest examples: maximum-entropy distributions, the norm-constrained view
of ridge regression, and a deliberately broken case at the edge of applicability where
the gradients refuse to align and the method silently fails.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
To minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g(\mathbf{x})=0$, you cannot just set
$\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ — the free minimum is off the surface. Instead, walk *along* the
constraint surface and stop where you can no longer improve without leaving it. That
happens exactly when **the gradient of $f$ has no component tangent to the surface** —
equivalently, when $\nabla f$ is **parallel** to the surface's normal direction
$\nabla g$:
$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) \;=\; \lambda\,\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star),
\qquad g(\mathbf{x}^\star)=0 .
$$
The proportionality constant $\lambda$ is the **Lagrange multiplier**. Bundle the two
conditions into one object, the **Lagrangian**
$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\lambda)=f(\mathbf{x})-\lambda\,g(\mathbf{x})$: setting *its*
gradient to zero in **all** variables — the $\mathbf{x}$'s and $\lambda$ — recovers the
parallelism (from the $\mathbf{x}$ equations) and the constraint itself (from the
$\lambda$ equation). The multiplier is not bookkeeping debris: $\lambda$ is a **shadow
price**, the sensitivity of the optimal value to relaxing the constraint.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The Lagrangian will look like a magic trick — fold a
constraint into the objective with a mystery coefficient, differentiate, and somehow get
the right answer. By the end you should see it as the *inevitable* bookkeeping of one
geometric fact: at the optimum no tangent direction goes downhill, and the only way for a
gradient to have no tangent component is for it to point straight along the normal.

# Why we are here: the fence the earlier articles never had {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the whole article: **why does the free derivative test
break the moment a constraint appears, and what is the smallest repair that fixes it?**
Every move below answers a piece of that.

## What changed

Through [OPTM-01] and [OPTM-02] the recipe never wavered: to minimize $f$, find where its
derivative (or gradient) is zero, then check curvature. That recipe rests on a quiet
assumption — that you are free to step in *any* direction. The condition
$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$ is precisely the statement "no direction is
downhill," and it is necessary only because *every* direction is available to you. A
nonzero gradient would hand you a descent direction $-\nabla f$, and you could simply
take it.

Constrained problems revoke that freedom. Now we want
$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \; f(\mathbf{x}) \quad\text{subject to}\quad g(\mathbf{x}) = 0,
$$ {#eq-problem}
where $g:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ carves out a **constraint surface** — the set of
points satisfying $g(\mathbf{x})=0$. The decision variable is no longer free to roam; it
is pinned to that surface. (We follow the convention $g(\mathbf{x})=0$; a constraint
written $g(\mathbf{x})=c$ is rewritten as $\tilde g(\mathbf{x})=g(\mathbf{x})-c=0$, so
"equals zero" loses no generality.)

These are not exotic. Every one of the following is @eq-problem in disguise:

- **Budget allocation.** Maximize utility (or output) $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to total
  spend $\sum_i p_i x_i = B$ — the budget line is the constraint surface.
- **Maximum likelihood for a multinomial.** Maximize a log-likelihood over probabilities
  $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ subject to $\sum_i x_i = 1$ — they must sum to one.
- **Portfolio variance.** Minimize risk $\mathbf{w}^\top\Sigma\mathbf{w}$ subject to
  fully-invested weights $\sum_i w_i = 1$.
- **The norm-constrained view of ridge regression.** Minimize squared error
  $\|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2$ subject to a budget on coefficient size
  $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 = t$ — the constraint that makes the ridge picture geometric
  (we carry this all the way through in @sec-ridge, the exact tie [REGR-14] links here for).

## Why the free test is now wrong

The naive fix would be: ignore the fence, minimize $f$ freely, and hope the answer lands
on the surface. It almost never does. @fig-why-free shows the simplest possible instance
— minimize $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ (distance-squared from the origin) on the line $x+y=2$. The
free minimum is the origin, which is *not* on the line; the constrained minimum is the
point on the line closest to the origin, and there the gradient $\nabla f$ does **not**
vanish. The free test would report "no minimum here," which is nonsense — there is a
perfectly good best point on the line. We need a different stopping rule.

```{python}
#| label: fig-why-free
#| fig-cap: "Why setting $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$ is the wrong test under a constraint. We minimize $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ (concentric circular level sets, blue) on the line $x+y=2$ (red). The free minimum is the origin (open marker), which is off the line. The constrained minimum (filled green) is where a level circle is *tangent* to the line — the smallest circle that still touches it. At that point $\\nabla f$ is nonzero; it points straight away from the line. The free first-order test never fires here."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.0))
# level sets of f
gx = np.linspace(-1.0, 3.0, 400)
gy = np.linspace(-1.0, 3.0, 400)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy)
F = GX**2 + GY**2
levels = [0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 6.0]
ax.contour(GX, GY, F, levels=levels, colors=CURVE, linewidths=1.0, alpha=0.7)
# constraint line x + y = 2
xs = np.linspace(-1.0, 3.0, 100)
ax.plot(xs, 2 - xs, color=CONSTR, lw=2.2, label="constraint $x+y=2$")
# constrained optimum: closest point on line to origin = (1,1)
ax.scatter([1], [1], color=GRADF, s=110, zorder=5, edgecolor="white",
           linewidth=1.4, label="constrained min $(1,1)$")
ax.scatter([0], [0], facecolor="white", edgecolor=CURVE, s=90, zorder=5,
           linewidth=1.8, label="free min $(0,0)$ — off the line")
# gradient of f at (1,1) = (2,2), points away from line
ax.annotate("", xy=(1 + 0.5, 1 + 0.5), xytext=(1, 1),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GRADF, lw=2.2))
ax.text(1.62, 1.55, r"$\nabla f$", color=GRADF, fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-1, 3); ax.set_ylim(-1, 3)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title("The constrained minimum is a tangency")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why can't we just substitute the constraint into the objective and minimize freely?"*
Sometimes you can, and when you can it is often the cleanest route — solve
$x+y=2$ for $y=2-x$, plug into $f$, and minimize the resulting one-variable function. The
multiplier method exists for the cases where substitution is ugly or impossible: when
the constraint cannot be solved explicitly for one variable (an implicit surface like
$x^2+y^2+z^2=1$ has no single clean "$z=$"), when there are many constraints tangled
together, or when you *want* the multiplier itself because it carries economic meaning
(the shadow price of @sec-shadow). Lagrange's method handles all constraints
symmetrically and never asks you to pick a variable to eliminate. We will check it
against substitution in @sec-entropy so you see the two agree.
:::

## The historical why

Joseph-Louis Lagrange introduced the method in his 1788 *Mécanique analytique*, recasting
all of mechanics as constrained optimization: a system's motion minimizes an "action"
subject to physical constraints (a bead must stay on its wire, a pendulum on its arc).
The multiplier was, from birth, a *force* — the constraint force the wire exerts to keep
the bead on track, exactly large enough to cancel any tendency to leave. That mechanical
origin is worth keeping in mind: $\lambda\nabla g$ is the "force" the constraint applies,
and the optimality condition $\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g$ says the objective's pull is
perfectly balanced by the constraint's push. The shadow-price reading in @sec-shadow is
the same idea wearing an economist's hat.

# Intuition before symbols: tangency {#sec-intuition}

We build the entire condition from one picture, no formulas yet. Stand on the constraint
surface $g(\mathbf{x})=0$ and imagine walking along it, watching the value of $f$. You are
at a constrained minimum when **no step you can take along the surface lowers $f$**.

Now overlay the **level sets** of $f$ — the curves (or surfaces) where $f$ equals a
constant, like the contour lines on a topographic map. Walking along the constraint
surface, you cross these contours; the value of $f$ you read off is the contour you are
on. You are decreasing $f$ as long as you are crossing into lower contours. You stop
improving exactly when the constraint surface stops crossing contours and instead
**grazes** one — when it is **tangent** to a level set of $f$. @fig-why-free showed this:
the optimum is where the smallest circle still touching the line touches it tangentially.

Here is why tangency *is* the optimality condition, in words before we make it algebra.
Recall the central fact from [OPTM-02]: the gradient $\nabla f$ points in the direction
of steepest *ascent* of $f$, and is perpendicular to $f$'s own level sets. Likewise
$\nabla g$ is perpendicular to the constraint surface $g=0$ (a level set of $g$). At a
tangency point, the level set of $f$ and the level set of $g$ touch and share the same
tangent line — so they share the same perpendicular direction. Therefore $\nabla f$ and
$\nabla g$ point along the *same line*: they are parallel. That single sentence is the
whole theorem; the rest of the article makes it precise and exploitable.

@fig-tangency-vs-not contrasts a non-optimal point (the gradients are misaligned, and you
can still slide downhill along the surface) with the optimal point (gradients aligned, no
tangential improvement left).

```{python}
#| label: fig-tangency-vs-not
#| fig-cap: "Tangency is optimality. **Left:** a non-optimal point on the constraint (red). The objective gradient $\\nabla f$ (green) makes an angle with the constraint normal $\\nabla g$ (gold); $\\nabla f$ has a component *along* the surface (dashed), so sliding along the constraint in the opposite tangential direction still lowers $f$. **Right:** the optimal point. $\\nabla f$ and $\\nabla g$ are parallel, $\\nabla f$ has *no* tangential component, and no along-surface step improves $f$ — the stopping rule of @sec-intuition."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.6))

def draw_panel(ax, x0, title):
    gx = np.linspace(-0.2, 3.2, 300); gy = np.linspace(-0.2, 3.2, 300)
    GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy)
    F = GX**2 + GY**2
    ax.contour(GX, GY, F, levels=[0.5,1,2,4,6,8], colors=CURVE, linewidths=0.9, alpha=0.6)
    xs = np.linspace(-0.2, 3.2, 100)
    ax.plot(xs, 2 - xs, color=CONSTR, lw=2.2)
    y0 = 2 - x0
    ax.scatter([x0], [y0], color=CONSTR, s=90, zorder=5, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.3)
    # grad f = (2x, 2y), grad g = (1,1)
    gf = np.array([2*x0, 2*y0]); gf = gf/np.linalg.norm(gf)*0.85
    gg = np.array([1.0, 1.0]); gg = gg/np.linalg.norm(gg)*0.85
    ax.annotate("", xy=(x0+gf[0], y0+gf[1]), xytext=(x0, y0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GRADF, lw=2.2))
    ax.annotate("", xy=(x0+gg[0], y0+gg[1]), xytext=(x0, y0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GRADG, lw=2.2))
    ax.text(x0+gf[0]+0.05, y0+gf[1], r"$\nabla f$", color=GRADF, fontsize=12)
    ax.text(x0+gg[0]+0.05, y0+gg[1]-0.18, r"$\nabla g$", color=GRADG, fontsize=12)
    # tangential component of grad f (project onto tangent direction (1,-1)/sqrt2)
    t = np.array([1.0, -1.0]); t = t/np.linalg.norm(t)
    gf_full = np.array([2*x0, 2*y0])
    comp = (gf_full @ t)
    if abs(comp) > 1e-9:
        tc = t*comp; tc = tc/np.linalg.norm(gf_full)*0.85
        ax.annotate("", xy=(x0+tc[0], y0+tc[1]), xytext=(x0, y0),
                    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GREY, lw=1.6, linestyle="--"))
        ax.text(x0+tc[0]-0.1, y0+tc[1]-0.25, "tangential\ncomponent", color=GREY, fontsize=8)
    ax.set_xlim(-0.2, 3.2); ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 3.2); ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$"); ax.set_title(title)

draw_panel(axL, 0.35, "Not optimal: gradients misaligned")
draw_panel(axR, 1.00, "Optimal: $\\nabla f \\parallel \\nabla g$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the multiplier $\lambda$ allowed to be negative — or zero?"* Parallel does not
mean "same direction." $\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g$ allows $\lambda<0$ (the gradients are
anti-parallel, pointing opposite ways along the shared normal line) and $\lambda=0$ (the
constraint is not "binding" in the gradient sense — $\nabla f$ already vanishes there, a
free stationary point that happens to sit on the surface). The *sign* of $\lambda$ will
matter enormously once constraints become inequalities in [OPTM-14], where a sign
condition $\mu\ge0$ separates the active from the inactive. For pure equalities, $\lambda$
is free to be any real number, and its sign tells you which way relaxing the constraint
moves the optimum — the shadow-price reading of @sec-shadow.
:::

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

## The geometric fact we stand on: $\nabla g \perp \{g=0\}$ {#sec-grad-perp}

The whole argument rests on one borrowed fact, so we state it carefully and motivate it
rather than wave at it. It is owned by the *Geometry of Data* track (level sets and the
gradient–level-set relationship); we import its statement and give the Calc-1-level reason
it is true.

::: {#lem-perp}
**Lemma (the gradient is normal to its level set).** Let $g:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ be
differentiable, and let $\mathbf{x}^\star$ be a point with $g(\mathbf{x}^\star)=0$. If
$\mathbf{r}(t)$ is any smooth curve lying entirely in the surface $g=0$ and passing
through $\mathbf{x}^\star$ at $t=0$, then the tangent vector $\mathbf{r}'(0)$ satisfies
$$
\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \mathbf{r}'(0) = 0 .
$$
That is, $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ is **perpendicular to every tangent direction of the
surface** at $\mathbf{x}^\star$ — it is the surface's normal vector.
:::

::: {.proof}
The curve stays on the surface, so $g(\mathbf{r}(t))=0$ for **all** $t$ near $0$ — it is
the constant function $0$. Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$. The left side is
a composition $t\mapsto g(\mathbf{r}(t))$, so the multivariable chain rule applies. That
rule — which itself is the one-variable chain rule applied coordinate by coordinate and
summed — states
$$
\frac{d}{dt}\,g(\mathbf{r}(t))
= \sum_{j=1}^n \frac{\partial g}{\partial x_j}\bigl(\mathbf{r}(t)\bigr)\,r_j'(t)
= \nabla g(\mathbf{r}(t))^\top \mathbf{r}'(t),
$$
because the sum of products $\sum_j (\partial g/\partial x_j)\,r_j'$ is exactly the dot
product of the gradient vector with the velocity vector. The right side is
$\tfrac{d}{dt}(0)=0$. Setting them equal and evaluating at $t=0$ (where
$\mathbf{r}(0)=\mathbf{x}^\star$),
$$
\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \mathbf{r}'(0) = 0 .
$$
Since $\mathbf{r}$ was an arbitrary on-surface curve through $\mathbf{x}^\star$, its
velocity $\mathbf{r}'(0)$ can point along any tangent direction of the surface. The
gradient is orthogonal to all of them. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The picture: the tangent directions of the surface form a flat $(n-1)$-dimensional slab
(the **tangent space**) at $\mathbf{x}^\star$, and $\nabla g$ is the one direction
sticking straight out of it. @fig-perp draws this for a circle in the plane.

```{python}
#| label: fig-perp
#| fig-cap: "The borrowed fact, @lem-perp, made visible. On the circle $g(x,y)=x^2+y^2-1=0$ (red), the gradient $\\nabla g=(2x,2y)$ (gold) points radially outward — exactly perpendicular to the tangent line of the circle (grey) at each point. Walk along the surface and your velocity is tangent; $\\nabla g$ is always the normal. This is the only above-floor fact the chapter imports, and the proof above is its full justification."
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 5.0))
ax.plot(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta), color=CONSTR, lw=2.2, label="$g(x,y)=x^2+y^2-1=0$")
for th in np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 8, endpoint=False):
    px, py = np.cos(th), np.sin(th)
    # gradient (radial)
    ax.annotate("", xy=(px*1.45, py*1.45), xytext=(px, py),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GRADG, lw=1.8))
    # tangent line segment
    tx, ty = -np.sin(th), np.cos(th)
    ax.plot([px-0.32*tx, px+0.32*tx], [py-0.32*ty, py+0.32*ty], color=GREY, lw=1.4)
ax.text(1.5, 0.05, r"$\nabla g$", color=GRADG, fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-1.7, 1.9); ax.set_ylim(-1.7, 1.7); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title(r"$\nabla g \perp$ the surface $g=0$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## From tangency to the multiplier equation {#sec-derive}

Now turn the picture into the equation. Let $\mathbf{x}^\star$ be a local minimizer of
@eq-problem and assume $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)\neq\mathbf{0}$ (this is the
**constraint qualification** — we return to *why* it is needed in @sec-failure, where it
fails). We argue in two steps.

**Step 1 — at the optimum, $\nabla f$ has no tangential component.** Take any smooth
on-surface curve $\mathbf{r}(t)$ with $\mathbf{r}(0)=\mathbf{x}^\star$ and tangent
$\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{r}'(0)$. The value of $f$ along this curve is the one-variable
function $\varphi(t)=f(\mathbf{r}(t))$. Because $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a local minimum of
$f$ *restricted to the surface*, and the curve lives on the surface, $t=0$ is a local
minimum of the ordinary one-variable function $\varphi$. By the Calc-1 first-order
condition, $\varphi'(0)=0$. Compute $\varphi'(0)$ with the same chain rule as in
@lem-perp:
$$
\varphi'(0)
= \frac{d}{dt}\,f(\mathbf{r}(t))\Big|_{t=0}
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \mathbf{r}'(0)
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \mathbf{v}.
$$
So $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top\mathbf{v}=0$ for **every** tangent direction
$\mathbf{v}$ of the surface. In words: $\nabla f$ is orthogonal to every tangent direction
— it has zero component along the surface. (This is the precise version of "no
along-surface step lowers $f$": the directional derivative of $f$ in every tangent
direction is zero.)

**Step 2 — orthogonal to every tangent direction means parallel to $\nabla g$.** We now
have two facts at $\mathbf{x}^\star$:

- $\nabla g$ is orthogonal to every tangent direction (@lem-perp), and it is the *only*
  such direction up to scaling — the tangent space is the $(n-1)$-dimensional set of all
  vectors orthogonal to the single nonzero vector $\nabla g$, so the set of vectors
  orthogonal to the *whole* tangent space is the one-dimensional line spanned by
  $\nabla g$.
- $\nabla f$ is also orthogonal to every tangent direction (Step 1).

Therefore $\nabla f$ lies in that one-dimensional line: it is a scalar multiple of
$\nabla g$. Naming the scalar $\lambda$,
$$
\boxed{\ \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \lambda\,\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star).\ }
$$ {#eq-stationarity}
That $\nabla g\neq\mathbf{0}$ is exactly what guarantees the orthogonal-complement is
one-dimensional (a single line, not a larger space) so that $\lambda$ exists and is unique;
this is where the constraint qualification earns its keep.

We have derived the Core's parallelism from nothing but the chain rule and the first-order
condition of Calc 1. No "it can be shown."

::: {#thm-lagrange}
**Theorem (Lagrange's first-order necessary condition).** Let $f,g:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$
be continuously differentiable and let $\mathbf{x}^\star$ be a local minimizer (or
maximizer) of $f$ subject to $g(\mathbf{x})=0$. If
$\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)\neq\mathbf{0}$, then there exists a scalar $\lambda^\star$ — the
**Lagrange multiplier** — such that
$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \lambda^\star\,\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star),
\qquad g(\mathbf{x}^\star)=0 .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The first equation is @eq-stationarity, derived in Steps 1–2 above. The second is just the
hypothesis that $\mathbf{x}^\star$ lies on the constraint surface. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

A word on what this is and is not. Like $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ in the unconstrained world,
@thm-lagrange is **necessary, not sufficient**: it flags every constrained minimum, but
also every constrained maximum and saddle. Whether a stationary point is a minimum needs a
second-order check (@sec-second-order) or a convexity argument (@sec-convex). And it
applies to maximization unchanged — maximizing $f$ is minimizing $-f$, and
$\nabla(-f)=-\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g$ is the same condition with $\lambda$ negated. So the
method finds constrained *stationary points* of either flavor; you sort them afterward.

## The Lagrangian: one function that remembers everything {#sec-lagrangian}

@thm-lagrange gives $n+1$ equations (the $n$ components of $\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g$, plus
$g=0$) in $n+1$ unknowns (the $n$ components of $\mathbf{x}$, plus $\lambda$). Lagrange's
genius was to repackage all $n+1$ as the *unconstrained* stationarity of a single scalar
function. Define the **Lagrangian**
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\lambda) \;=\; f(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda\, g(\mathbf{x}),
$$ {#eq-lagrangian}
a function of the original variables $\mathbf{x}$ *and* the new variable $\lambda$, which
we promote to a genuine coordinate. Now set its full gradient — with respect to **all**
$n+1$ variables — to zero, and watch the two conditions of @thm-lagrange fall out.

**The $\mathbf{x}$-block.** Differentiating @eq-lagrangian with respect to the components
of $\mathbf{x}$ (treating $\lambda$ as a constant, since it is held fixed in this block):
$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}
= \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\bigl[f(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda g(\mathbf{x})\bigr]
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda\,\nabla g(\mathbf{x}).
$$
Setting this to $\mathbf{0}$ gives precisely $\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g$ — the parallelism.

**The $\lambda$-block.** Differentiating @eq-lagrangian with respect to $\lambda$
(treating $\mathbf{x}$ as constant, so $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a constant and contributes
nothing, while $-\lambda g(\mathbf{x})$ has $\lambda$-derivative $-g(\mathbf{x})$):
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda}
= -\,g(\mathbf{x}).
$$
Setting this to $0$ gives $g(\mathbf{x})=0$ — the constraint itself, recovered for free.
This is the slick part: the constraint is enforced not by hand but as the
$\lambda$-stationarity of $\mathcal{L}$. Together:
$$
\boxed{\
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}=\mathbf{0}
\ \ \text{and}\ \
\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial\lambda}=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\nabla f = \lambda\nabla g
\ \ \text{and}\ \
g=0.
\ }
$$ {#eq-lagrangian-conditions}

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The sign and the saddle.** Some texts write $\mathcal{L}=f+\lambda g$; we write
$f-\lambda g$. Either is fine — they differ only by the sign of $\lambda$, and since
$\lambda$ is solved for, the *point* $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is identical. But be consistent
within a problem, and know your convention when you read off the multiplier's sign for the
shadow-price interpretation (@sec-shadow). The second trap: a solution of
@eq-lagrangian-conditions is a **stationary point of $\mathcal{L}$**, but it is **not a
minimum of $\mathcal{L}$** in the ordinary sense. In fact $(\mathbf{x}^\star,\lambda^\star)$
is typically a **saddle point** of $\mathcal{L}$ — a minimum in $\mathbf{x}$ and a maximum
in $\lambda$ (this min–max structure is the whole subject of duality, [OPTM-15]). Do
**not** try to minimize $\mathcal{L}$ jointly over $(\mathbf{x},\lambda)$; you will run
$\lambda$ off to $\pm\infty$. Solve the stationarity *equations*, do not descend on
$\mathcal{L}$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Walk me through solving a Lagrange-multiplier problem on a whiteboard."* The four-step
ritual, which the worked examples follow verbatim:
1. **Write the Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L}=f-\lambda g$ (move any constraint to the
   $g=0$ form first).
2. **Set $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}=\mathbf{0}$** — one equation per original variable.
3. **Set $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\lambda=0$** — this just re-states the constraint
   $g=0$.
4. **Solve the system** of $n+1$ equations for $(\mathbf{x},\lambda)$, then check
   second-order conditions (or convexity) to confirm min vs. max.
The most common interview slip is forgetting step 3 (so you have $n$ equations in $n+1$
unknowns and cannot solve) or trying to minimize $\mathcal{L}$ numerically instead of
solving the stationarity system.
:::

## Several equality constraints {#sec-multiple}

Real problems fence the variable with more than one wall. Suppose $m$ equality
constraints, $g_1(\mathbf{x})=0,\dots,g_m(\mathbf{x})=0$ (with $m<n$, or there is no room
to move). The feasible surface is now the intersection of $m$ surfaces. The geometry
generalizes cleanly: a tangent direction of the intersection must stay on *all* $m$
surfaces at once, so by @lem-perp it must be orthogonal to *every* $\nabla g_k$. The
optimality argument of @sec-derive then says $\nabla f$ is orthogonal to every such
tangent direction — and the set of vectors orthogonal to all the tangent directions is now
the *span* of the normals $\nabla g_1,\dots,\nabla g_m$. Hence $\nabla f$ is a linear
combination of them:
$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \sum_{k=1}^m \lambda_k\,\nabla g_k(\mathbf{x}^\star),
$$ {#eq-multi-stationarity}
one multiplier $\lambda_k$ per constraint. (The clean version of this argument needs the
$\nabla g_k$ to be **linearly independent** at $\mathbf{x}^\star$ — the general constraint
qualification, the multi-constraint cousin of "$\nabla g\neq\mathbf{0}$." If the normals
were dependent, the span would be too small to be sure $\nabla f$ lands in it.) The
Lagrangian absorbs all of them:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda)
= f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{k=1}^m \lambda_k\, g_k(\mathbf{x}),
$$
and $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}=\mathbf{0}$ reproduces @eq-multi-stationarity while the
$m$ derivatives $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\lambda_k=-g_k=0$ reproduce all $m$
constraints. Nothing new conceptually — just one multiplier per fence.

## Second-order conditions: is it a minimum? {#sec-second-order}

@thm-lagrange is necessary; to *confirm* a minimum we need curvature, and — this is the
subtlety — only the curvature **along the surface** matters. The relevant object is the
Hessian of the Lagrangian in $\mathbf{x}$,
$$
\nabla^2_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}
= \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star) - \sum_k \lambda_k\,\nabla^2 g_k(\mathbf{x}^\star),
$$
restricted to tangent directions. The bordered/second-order condition says
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a (strict) local constrained minimum if
$$
\mathbf{v}^\top \bigl(\nabla^2_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}\bigr)\,\mathbf{v} > 0
\quad\text{for every nonzero tangent direction } \mathbf{v}
\ \ (\text{i.e. } \nabla g_k^\top\mathbf{v}=0 \ \forall k).
$$
Why the Lagrangian's Hessian and not $f$'s alone? Because moving along a *curved*
constraint surface bends your path, and that bending interacts with $f$'s slope; the
$-\sum\lambda_k\nabla^2 g_k$ term is exactly the correction that accounts for the
surface's curvature. Why only tangent directions? Because you are forbidden from moving
off the surface, so curvature in the normal direction is irrelevant — you will never step
that way. We will not belabor the general bordered-Hessian determinant test here; in the
convex case (next) a far cleaner sufficient condition holds, and that is the case that
covers our examples and most of machine learning.

## When stationary $\Rightarrow$ global: the convex case {#sec-convex}

The clean and practically dominant situation: if $f$ is **convex** and each constraint is
**affine** (linear plus a constant, $g_k(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{a}_k^\top\mathbf{x}-b_k$),
then the feasible set is convex (an intersection of hyperplanes, [OPTM-03]) and any point
satisfying @eq-lagrangian-conditions is a **global** constrained minimum — no second-order
test needed. The reason is the headline theorem of [OPTM-04]: for a convex objective over
a convex feasible set, stationarity is sufficient for the global minimum, because the
tangent-below property of a convex function forbids any other point from being lower. This
is why constrained convex problems — least squares with linear constraints, maximum
entropy, ridge, the SVM — are the well-behaved heart of the field, and why every example
we carry through is convex. The honest boundary (non-convex constraints, where Lagrange
finds a stationary point that need not be global) waits in @sec-failure.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the four-step ritual on three honest problems, the third deliberately at the edge
where the method strains.

## Example 1 — maximum entropy subject to a fixed mean {#sec-entropy}

The maximum-entropy principle is everywhere in statistics and machine learning: among all
distributions consistent with what you know, pick the one that assumes the least — the one
with maximum **entropy** $H(\mathbf{p})=-\sum_i p_i\ln p_i$. Take a die-like variable on
outcomes $1,\dots,K$ with probabilities $\mathbf{p}=(p_1,\dots,p_K)$. We *know* two things:
the probabilities sum to one, and the mean outcome equals a target $\mu$. Find the
maximum-entropy distribution. This is genuinely how one derives that the maximum-entropy
distribution with a fixed mean is **exponential / Boltzmann** in form — a result that
underlies softmax, Gibbs distributions, and logistic models.

**Setup.** Maximize $H(\mathbf{p})=-\sum_{i=1}^K p_i\ln p_i$ subject to
$$
g_1(\mathbf{p}) = \sum_{i=1}^K p_i - 1 = 0,
\qquad
g_2(\mathbf{p}) = \sum_{i=1}^K i\,p_i - \mu = 0 .
$$
(Maximizing $H$ is minimizing $-H$; we maximize directly and let $\lambda$ sort the sign.)

**Step 1 — Lagrangian** (two constraints, two multipliers $\lambda,\nu$):
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{p},\lambda,\nu)
= -\sum_i p_i\ln p_i
\;-\; \lambda\Bigl(\sum_i p_i - 1\Bigr)
\;-\; \nu\Bigl(\sum_i i\,p_i - \mu\Bigr).
$$

**Step 2 — stationarity in each $p_i$.** Differentiate with respect to a single $p_i$.
Only the $i$-th term of each sum involves $p_i$. The derivative of $-p_i\ln p_i$ is
$-(\ln p_i + p_i\cdot\tfrac1{p_i}) = -\ln p_i - 1$ (product rule on $p_i$ and $\ln p_i$).
The derivatives of the constraint sums in $p_i$ are $1$ and $i$ respectively. So
$$
\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial p_i}
= -\ln p_i - 1 - \lambda - \nu\, i = 0.
$$
Solve for $p_i$ by isolating the logarithm and exponentiating:
$$
\ln p_i = -1 - \lambda - \nu\, i
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_i = e^{-1-\lambda}\;e^{-\nu i}.
$$
Write $Z = e^{1+\lambda}$ so that $p_i = \tfrac{1}{Z}e^{-\nu i}$ — an **exponential family
in the outcome value $i$**. The multiplier $\lambda$ became the (log) normalizer; the
multiplier $\nu$ became the rate parameter that tilts the distribution toward the target
mean. *Maximum entropy with a mean constraint forces an exponential form* — derived, not
asserted.

**Step 3 — the constraints (the $\lambda,\nu$ equations).** $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\lambda=0$
and $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\nu=0$ re-state $\sum_i p_i=1$ and $\sum_i i\,p_i=\mu$. The
first pins $Z=\sum_i e^{-\nu i}$ (normalization); the second pins $\nu$ so the mean comes
out to $\mu$.

**Step 4 — solve numerically and confirm.** $-H$ is convex on the probability simplex (a
sum of convex $p\ln p$ terms) and the constraints are affine, so by @sec-convex the
stationary point is the global maximum-entropy distribution. We solve for $\nu$ so the mean
hits $\mu$, then plot.

```{python}
#| label: fig-maxent
#| fig-cap: "Maximum-entropy distributions on $\\{1,\\dots,6\\}$ for three target means $\\mu$. Each is the exponential form $p_i \\propto e^{-\\nu i}$ that the Lagrange conditions forced. With $\\mu=3.5$ (the unconstrained mean of a fair die) the rate $\\nu=0$ and the distribution is **uniform** — maximum entropy with no informative constraint. Pulling $\\mu$ below $3.5$ tilts mass toward low outcomes ($\\nu>0$); above $3.5$, toward high outcomes ($\\nu<0$). The multiplier $\\nu$ *is* the tilt."
from scipy.optimize import brentq

K = 6
i_vals = np.arange(1, K + 1)

def mean_for_nu(nu):
    w = np.exp(-nu * i_vals)
    p = w / w.sum()
    return (i_vals * p).sum()

def dist_for_mean(mu):
    # find nu with mean = mu; nu=0 gives mean 3.5
    nu = brentq(lambda nu: mean_for_nu(nu) - mu, -5, 5)
    w = np.exp(-nu * i_vals)
    return w / w.sum(), nu

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
width = 0.26
for k, (mu, col) in enumerate(zip([2.5, 3.5, 4.5], [GRADF, CURVE, CONSTR])):
    p, nu = dist_for_mean(mu)
    H = -(p * np.log(p)).sum()
    ax.bar(i_vals + (k - 1) * width, p, width=width, color=col, alpha=0.85,
           label=fr"$\mu={mu}$, $\nu={nu:.2f}$, $H={H:.3f}$")
ax.set_xlabel("outcome $i$"); ax.set_ylabel("probability $p_i$")
ax.set_xticks(i_vals)
ax.set_title("Max-entropy distributions are exponential in the outcome")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

This is the **substitution-check** promised earlier: with only the normalization
constraint (drop the mean), the same Lagrangian gives $p_i=e^{-1-\lambda}$ — *constant in
$i$* — so $p_i=1/K$, the uniform distribution. Maximum entropy with nothing but
"probabilities sum to one" is the uniform distribution, which you could also get by direct
substitution; the multiplier method recovers it and generalizes to the mean-constrained
case where substitution is far messier.

## Example 2 — ridge regression as a norm-constrained least-squares problem {#sec-ridge}

This is the exact link [REGR-14] requests. Ridge regression is usually written as a
*penalized* problem, $\min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 +
\alpha\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2$. But it has a twin **constrained** form that makes its
geometry transparent: minimize squared error subject to a *budget* on coefficient size,
$$
\min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\ \|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 = t .
$$ {#eq-ridge-constrained}
Lagrange multipliers turn the constrained form into the penalized form and reveal the
penalty weight $\alpha$ to be *the multiplier itself*.

**Step 1 — Lagrangian** with $g(\boldsymbol\beta)=\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 - t$:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\beta,\lambda)
= \|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 - \lambda\bigl(\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 - t\bigr).
$$

**Step 2 — stationarity in $\boldsymbol\beta$.** We need the two gradients. From [OPTM-02]
(and re-derived there in full), $\nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2
= -2X^\top(\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta)$ and $\nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2
= 2\boldsymbol\beta$. So
$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\mathcal{L}
= -2X^\top(\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta) - \lambda\,(2\boldsymbol\beta) = \mathbf{0}.
$$
Divide by $2$ and rearrange — set $\alpha=-\lambda$ to land on the standard sign:
$$
X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta + \alpha\,\boldsymbol\beta = X^\top\mathbf{y}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
(X^\top X + \alpha I)\,\boldsymbol\beta = X^\top\mathbf{y}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\ \hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = (X^\top X + \alpha I)^{-1}X^\top\mathbf{y}.\ }
$$ {#eq-ridge-soln}
That is **exactly the ridge estimator.** The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ (up to sign)
*is* the ridge penalty weight $\alpha$. The constrained budget $t$ and the penalty $\alpha$
are two parameterizations of one knob: a tighter budget (smaller $t$) corresponds to a
heavier penalty (larger $\alpha$), and @sec-shadow makes that trade-off quantitative.

**Step 3 — the constraint** $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2=t$ ties $\alpha$ to $t$: the multiplier
adjusts until the solution's norm equals the budget. **Step 4:** the objective is convex
(a sum of squares) and the feasible set $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2\le t$ is a convex ball, so
the stationary point is the global constrained minimum (@sec-convex). @fig-ridge draws the
tangency that @eq-ridge-soln encodes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ridge
#| fig-cap: "Ridge as a constrained problem, in the $(\\beta_1,\\beta_2)$ plane. Blue ellipses are level sets of the squared-error objective $\\|\\mathbf{y}-X\\boldsymbol\\beta\\|^2$, centered at the unconstrained OLS solution (open marker). The red circle is the coefficient budget $\\|\\boldsymbol\\beta\\|^2=t$. The constrained optimum (green) is where the smallest error-ellipse is **tangent** to the budget circle — exactly the Lagrange condition $\\nabla f \\parallel \\nabla g$, with $\\nabla g$ (gold) pointing radially. Shrinking the budget slides the optimum toward the origin: that is shrinkage."
# A small, well-conditioned design so the picture is clean.
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
Xd = rng.normal(size=(40, 2))
Xd[:, 1] += 0.4 * Xd[:, 0]
beta_true = np.array([1.6, -0.9])
yd = Xd @ beta_true + rng.normal(0, 0.5, size=40)
XtX = Xd.T @ Xd
Xty = Xd.T @ yd
beta_ols = np.linalg.solve(XtX, Xty)

t = 1.3  # budget radius^2
alpha = 6.0
beta_ridge = np.linalg.solve(XtX + alpha * np.eye(2), Xty)

b1 = np.linspace(-1.5, 2.6, 300)
b2 = np.linspace(-2.4, 1.6, 300)
B1, B2 = np.meshgrid(b1, b2)
# SSE(beta) = (beta-beta_ols)^T XtX (beta-beta_ols) + const
D1 = B1 - beta_ols[0]; D2 = B2 - beta_ols[1]
SSE = XtX[0,0]*D1**2 + 2*XtX[0,1]*D1*D2 + XtX[1,1]*D2**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 5.2))
ax.contour(B1, B2, SSE, levels=12, colors=CURVE, linewidths=0.8, alpha=0.6)
th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
r = np.sqrt(t)
ax.plot(r*np.cos(th), r*np.sin(th), color=CONSTR, lw=2.2, label=r"budget $\|\beta\|^2=t$")
ax.scatter([beta_ols[0]], [beta_ols[1]], facecolor="white", edgecolor=CURVE,
           s=90, linewidth=1.8, zorder=5, label="OLS (free min)")
ax.scatter([beta_ridge[0]], [beta_ridge[1]], color=GRADF, s=110, zorder=6,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.4, label="ridge (constrained min)")
# grad g at ridge point = 2*beta_ridge (radial)
gg = beta_ridge / np.linalg.norm(beta_ridge) * 0.7
ax.annotate("", xy=(beta_ridge[0]+gg[0], beta_ridge[1]+gg[1]),
            xytext=(beta_ridge[0], beta_ridge[1]),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GRADG, lw=2.0))
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_1$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_2$")
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("Ridge: error-ellipse tangent to the budget ball")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Lasso swaps the $\ell_2$ budget for an $\ell_1$ one. Does Lagrange still apply?"* The
*constraint* picture survives — minimize squared error subject to
$\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1\le t$ is the lasso budget — but the equality machinery of *this*
article does not, for two reasons. First, the binding lasso constraint is an
**inequality** $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1\le t$, which needs the KKT generalization of
[OPTM-14]. Second, $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1$ is **non-differentiable** at the axes
(its corners), so $\nabla g$ does not exist there — and that is *precisely* why lasso
produces sparse solutions: the optimum loves to sit on a corner where a coefficient is
exactly zero. The kinked-constraint case is exactly what subgradients ([OPTM-05]) and the
KKT conditions ([OPTM-14]) were built for. So this is a clean example of the *edge* of
equality-Lagrange applicability.
:::

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: when the gradients refuse to align {#sec-edge}

Every honest treatment shows where the method fails. @thm-lagrange carried a hypothesis we
have so far taken for granted: $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)\neq\mathbf{0}$ (the constraint
qualification). When it fails, the parallelism equation can have **no solution at the true
optimum**, and a student who blindly writes the Lagrangian gets a *wrong* or *empty*
answer. The classic teaching example, due to constraint-qualification folklore:
$$
\min\ f(x,y) = x
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
g(x,y) = y^2 - x^3 = 0 .
$$
The constraint curve is a **cusp** at the origin (the semicubical parabola): $y=\pm
x^{3/2}$, which comes to a sharp point at $(0,0)$. The true constrained minimum of
$f=x$ — the smallest $x$ on the curve — is at the cusp, $\mathbf{x}^\star=(0,0)$, since
$x\ge0$ everywhere on the curve.

Now run the ritual and watch it break. $\nabla f=(1,0)$ everywhere. $\nabla g=(-3x^2,\,2y)$,
which at the origin is $(-3\cdot0,\,2\cdot0)=(0,0)=\mathbf{0}$. The Lagrange condition
$\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g$ becomes
$$
(1,0) = \lambda\,(0,0) = (0,0),
$$
which is **impossible** for any $\lambda$ — the first component demands $1=0$. The method
reports "no stationary point" at the very location that is the true optimum. The failure is
not arithmetic; it is geometric: at a cusp the surface has no well-defined tangent
direction and no well-defined normal, so the entire "$\nabla f$ has no tangential
component $\Rightarrow$ parallel to the normal" argument of @sec-derive collapses — there
is no normal to be parallel to.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: a cusp constraint where Lagrange fails. The curve $y^2=x^3$ (red) has a sharp point at the origin. The true minimizer of $f(x,y)=x$ on the curve is the cusp $(0,0)$ — the leftmost point (green). But $\\nabla g=(-3x^2,2y)$ **vanishes** there (no normal direction exists), so the condition $\\nabla f=\\lambda\\nabla g$ — i.e. $(1,0)=\\lambda(0,0)$ — has no solution. The constraint qualification $\\nabla g\\neq\\mathbf{0}$ is exactly the hypothesis that rules out this pathology."
xv = np.linspace(0, 1.6, 400)
yv = xv**1.5
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 4.6))
ax.plot(xv, yv, color=CONSTR, lw=2.2)
ax.plot(xv, -yv, color=CONSTR, lw=2.2, label=r"constraint $y^2=x^3$")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=GRADF, s=120, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
           linewidth=1.4, label="true min (cusp)")
# grad f arrow (constant)
ax.annotate("", xy=(0.35, 0.0), xytext=(0.0, 0.0),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GRADF, lw=2.2))
ax.text(0.37, 0.05, r"$\nabla f=(1,0)$", color=GRADF, fontsize=11)
ax.text(0.05, -1.4, r"$\nabla g=(0,0)$ at the cusp:" "\n" r"no normal, no $\lambda$ works",
        color=GREY, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlim(-0.15, 1.6); ax.set_ylim(-2.0, 2.0)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title("Constraint qualification fails at a cusp")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**What you reach for instead.** When the constraint qualification fails, the multiplier
form is the wrong instrument. The honest moves are: (i) recognize the geometry — a
cusp, a self-intersection, a tangency of constraints — and handle the bad point separately
(here, just evaluate $f$ at the cusp directly); (ii) reparameterize the curve to remove the
cusp ($x=s^2, y=s^3$ traces the same curve smoothly, and $f=x=s^2$ is trivially minimized
at $s=0$); or (iii) use the more general optimality conditions (the Fritz John conditions,
or KKT with a constraint qualification check in [OPTM-14]) that are built to flag exactly
this case. The lesson for practice: **always verify $\nabla g\neq\mathbf{0}$ at your
candidate before trusting a multiplier.** In smooth, well-posed problems — including every
convex problem with affine constraints, where $\nabla g_k$ are constant nonzero vectors —
this never bites; it bites on degenerate, non-smooth, or tangent-constraint geometry.

# The multiplier is a shadow price {#sec-shadow}

We promised the interpretation that makes duality click, and it is worth a section of its
own because it is *the* reason practitioners care about $\lambda$ beyond solving the
problem. The claim: **$\lambda^\star$ measures how much the optimal value would improve if
we relaxed the constraint by one unit.** In economics that is a *price* — the value of one
more unit of a scarce resource — hence "shadow price." Let us derive it, every step.

Generalize the constraint to a movable level, $g(\mathbf{x})=c$, and let $\mathbf{x}^\star(c)$
be the optimizer and $p^\star(c)=f(\mathbf{x}^\star(c))$ the **optimal value as a function
of the constraint level** $c$. We compute how $p^\star$ changes with $c$.

By the chain rule, differentiating $p^\star(c)=f(\mathbf{x}^\star(c))$ with respect to $c$:
$$
\frac{d p^\star}{dc}
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star(c))^\top \frac{d\mathbf{x}^\star}{dc}.
$$
At the optimum, the Lagrange condition (now with the constraint $g(\mathbf{x})-c=0$, whose
gradient in $\mathbf{x}$ is still $\nabla g$) gives $\nabla f=\lambda^\star\nabla g$.
Substitute:
$$
\frac{d p^\star}{dc}
= \lambda^\star\,\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \frac{d\mathbf{x}^\star}{dc}.
$$
Now use the constraint itself. The optimizer stays feasible as $c$ moves, so
$g(\mathbf{x}^\star(c))=c$ holds for all $c$. Differentiate *that* identity with respect to
$c$ — left side by the chain rule, right side is just $1$:
$$
\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \frac{d\mathbf{x}^\star}{dc} = 1 .
$$
This is the key cancellation: the awkward $d\mathbf{x}^\star/dc$ term, paired with
$\nabla g$, collapses to exactly $1$. Substitute back:
$$
\boxed{\ \frac{d p^\star}{dc} = \lambda^\star.\ }
$$ {#eq-shadow}
The multiplier is the **derivative of the optimal value with respect to the constraint
level** — the sensitivity, the marginal value, the shadow price. (We have shown this for a
single constraint; with several, $\partial p^\star/\partial c_k=\lambda_k^\star$ by the
identical argument, one constraint at a time.)

Read what @eq-shadow buys you:

- **It gives the multiplier a unit and a meaning.** In the budget problem, $\lambda$ is
  "utility per dollar of budget" — relax the budget by one dollar and the optimal utility
  rises by about $\lambda$. In ridge (@sec-ridge), it quantifies how much squared error you
  pay (or save) per unit of coefficient budget — the exact slope of the error-vs-budget
  trade-off.
- **Its sign is informative.** A positive $\lambda^\star$ means loosening the constraint
  *helps* the objective; the constraint is "expensive." Zero means the constraint is not
  binding at the margin. This sign logic is precisely what hardens into the
  dual-feasibility condition $\mu\ge0$ for *inequality* constraints in [OPTM-14].
- **It is the seed of duality.** Reading $\lambda$ as the price of a constraint is the
  doorway to the dual problem of [OPTM-15], where you *optimize over the prices* and
  discover the dual is a complete shadow economy of the primal.

@fig-shadow checks @eq-shadow numerically on the simple problem of @fig-why-free: vary the
constraint level $c$ in $x+y=c$, plot the optimal value, and confirm its slope is the
multiplier.

```{python}
#| label: fig-shadow
#| fig-cap: "The shadow-price identity @eq-shadow, verified. For $\\min x^2+y^2$ subject to $x+y=c$, the constrained optimum is $(c/2,c/2)$ with optimal value $p^\\star(c)=c^2/2$ (blue curve). Its derivative $dp^\\star/dc=c$ (slope of the tangent, dashed) equals the Lagrange multiplier $\\lambda^\\star=c$ solved at each $c$ (gold dots). The multiplier *is* the marginal value of relaxing the constraint, exactly as derived."
c_vals = np.linspace(0.5, 4.0, 200)
p_star = c_vals**2 / 2  # optimal value
# multiplier: grad f = (2x,2y)=(c,c); grad g=(1,1); lambda = c
lam = c_vals.copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.2))
ax.plot(c_vals, p_star, color=CURVE, lw=2.2, label=r"optimal value $p^\star(c)=c^2/2$")
# tangent at c0
c0 = 2.0
p0 = c0**2/2
slope = c0
tt = np.linspace(c0 - 0.9, c0 + 0.9, 50)
ax.plot(tt, p0 + slope*(tt - c0), color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6,
        label=fr"tangent slope $=\lambda^\star={slope:.0f}$ at $c={c0:.0f}$")
# sample multiplier dots
cs = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
ax.scatter(cs, cs**2/2, color=GRADG, s=70, zorder=5,
           label=r"$\lambda^\star(c)=dp^\star/dc$")
ax.set_xlabel("constraint level $c$ (in $x+y=c$)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"optimal value $p^\star$")
ax.set_title(r"Multiplier $=$ slope of optimal value vs. constraint")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"A constraint has multiplier $\lambda^\star = 0$ at the optimum — what does that tell
you?"* That the constraint is **not binding at the margin**: relaxing it would not improve
the objective, because $\nabla f$ already vanishes (a free stationary point happens to lie
on the surface) or the optimum would not move. For equality constraints this is unusual but
possible. The reason the question matters is its sequel: for *inequality* constraints
([OPTM-14]), a zero multiplier is the signature of an **inactive** constraint, and the
condition "multiplier zero **or** constraint active" is **complementary slackness**, the
heart of KKT. The shadow-price reading you derived here is what makes that condition
intuitive rather than a rule to memorize.
:::

# Failure modes and when to reach for something else {#sec-failure}

Pulling the boundaries together, since judgment is the goal.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Lagrange finds stationary points, not minima — and only if the geometry is clean.** The
three ways the method misleads, in order of how often they bite:

1. **It does not tell min from max from saddle.** @thm-lagrange is *necessary only*. You
   must sort the stationary points with a second-order check (@sec-second-order),
   convexity (@sec-convex), or by simply evaluating $f$ at each candidate. Reporting the
   first solution of the stationarity system as "the minimum" is the most common error.
2. **The constraint qualification can fail** (@sec-edge): if $\nabla g=\mathbf{0}$ at the
   optimum (a cusp, a self-intersection) or the multiple-constraint normals are linearly
   dependent, the parallelism equation may have no solution at the true optimum, and the
   method silently misses it. Always check $\nabla g\neq\mathbf{0}$ at your candidate.
3. **It assumes equality and smoothness.** Inequality constraints
   ($g(\mathbf{x})\le0$) need KKT ([OPTM-14]); non-differentiable constraints (an $\ell_1$
   ball, a polytope's corner) need subgradients ([OPTM-05]) or KKT. Equality Lagrange is
   the *smooth-surface, two-sided-fence* tool; outside that it is the wrong instrument.
:::

**Where it shines.** Smooth objective, smooth equality constraints with nonvanishing
gradients, and — ideally — convex objective with affine constraints, where stationarity is
*global* and the answer is the genuine minimum. That regime covers an enormous amount:
linearly-constrained least squares, maximum-entropy and exponential-family derivations, the
$\ell_2$/ridge geometry, equality-constrained quadratic programs, portfolio optimization
with a budget, and the analytical backbone of physics and economics.

**Where it does not, and the alternative:**

- **Inequalities** ($\le$, $\ge$): use the **KKT conditions** ([OPTM-14]) — the direct
  generalization, with one new ingredient (a sign condition and complementary slackness).
- **Non-smooth constraints / penalties** (the $\ell_1$ ball, kinks): **subgradients**
  ([OPTM-05]) and **proximal/projected methods** ([OPTM-16]).
- **Large-scale numerical problems** where you cannot solve the stationarity system by
  hand: feed the Lagrangian to a numerical solver — augmented-Lagrangian / ADMM methods,
  or interior-point methods — which are built on exactly this stationarity but iterate
  toward it. The hand-method here is the *theory*; those are the *engines*.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the **on-ramp and the two spines**: [OPTM-02] taught the unconstrained
test $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ and the gradient-as-steepest-ascent reading that this article
amended for the constrained world. This is the first of the four **constrained-optimization**
articles, and it establishes the geometric and algebraic core the other three extend:

- **[OPTM-14 *Inequality Constraints and the KKT Conditions*]** — the fence becomes
  one-sided, $g(\mathbf{x})\le0$. The parallelism survives but gains a **sign condition**
  ($\mu\ge0$) and **complementary slackness** ($\mu g=0$) — both of which the shadow-price
  reading (@sec-shadow) makes inevitable rather than arbitrary. This is the optimality
  backbone the SVM (Classification) links here for.
- **[OPTM-15 *Duality*]** — the multiplier $\lambda$, re-read as a *price*, is promoted to
  the decision variable of a twin problem. The min–max saddle structure of the Lagrangian
  (the Pitfall in @sec-lagrangian) is exactly the primal–dual relationship.
- **[OPTM-16 *Proximal and Projected Methods*]** — when the constraint set is non-smooth or
  you must iterate numerically, projection and proximal operators replace the hand-solved
  stationarity system.

Downstream of the track, the **ridge derivation** (@sec-ridge) is the exact constrained-vs-
penalized equivalence [REGR-14] links here for, and the **maximum-entropy / exponential-form**
result (@sec-entropy) underlies the softmax and Gibbs distributions across Classification
and Unsupervised Learning.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary source.** Lagrange, *Mécanique analytique* (1788) — the method born as the
  mechanics of constraint forces, where the multiplier *is* a physical force. The modern
  optimization framing traces through the 20th-century work that became the KKT conditions
  ([OPTM-14]).
- **The standard reference.** Boyd & Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization*, Ch. 5 (duality),
  whose treatment of the Lagrangian, the shadow-price/sensitivity reading, and the move to
  KKT is the path [OPTM-14] and [OPTM-15] follow.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What about one-sided constraints?*
  — the KKT conditions, [OPTM-14]. *Why does the Lagrangian's saddle structure matter, and
  can I solve the dual instead of the primal?* — duality, [OPTM-15], where weak/strong
  duality and Slater's condition close the loop. *What is the rigorous statement and proof
  of the constraint qualification and the second-order (bordered Hessian) conditions?* —
  any graduate text on nonlinear programming (Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization*,
  Ch. 12); we used the conditions honestly but did not prove the bordered-Hessian test in
  full, flagging it as the one second-order result we stated and motivated rather than
  derived end-to-end.
